abstract: Document-level sentiment classification remains a significant challenge due to the difficulty of effectively encoding the complex semantic and syntactic relationships between sentences. Traditional approaches often rely on sparse, discrete features or local n-gram information, failing to capture the global coherence and discourse relations—such as contrast or causality—that determine a document's overall polarity. In this paper, we propose a unified, bottom-up neural network framework to learn continuous document representations. Our model follows a two-stage composition process: it first generates sentence-level representations using either Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) or Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, and then employs a Gated Recurrent Neural Network (GRNN) to adaptively integrate these sentence vectors into a final document-level representation. By utilizing neural gates, the model is able to learn which information to preserve or discard across long sequences, effectively addressing the vanishing gradient problem inherent in standard recurrent models. We evaluate our approach on four large-scale review datasets from IMDB and the Yelp Dataset Challenge. The experimental results demonstrate that our model consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, including feature-based SVMs and Paragraph Vector. Furthermore, we show that the inclusion of gating mechanisms in the document composition layer provides a substantial performance boost over standard recurrent architectures.
